video_title: The Missing Barrels That Could Reprice Oil | Global Macro | Ep.105
gist: Natural-resource investor Adam Rosenwik argues the Strait of Hormuz closure caused a real ~1-1.5 billion barrel physical oil shock (unlike 2022's Russia scare), that the market is only halfway through a lagged inventory collapse that could reach a breaking point by late summer, and that the single biggest mispriced opportunity today is upstream natural gas to power AI data centers -- while his fund has rotated out of gold into energy.

themes.theme: The Capex Cycle Framework Behind Commodity Supercycles

insights.claim: Commodity supercycles are driven by a multi-year capex cycle: prices rise while a deficit persists, eventually overshoot past the equilibrium level of new investment, then a wave of new supply comes online and prices collapse, wiping out investors until depletion eventually restores balance.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Investors can use this capex-cycle framework to judge where in the commodity cycle a given resource sits, rather than reacting to short-term price moves.
insights.quote: I think in our industry, we have a sea star.

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik argues the current commodity upcycle actually began in 2020 at the COVID-era lows, not in 2022 with the Russia-Ukraine war or in 2025 with the Iran/Strait of Hormuz conflict; geopolitical shocks are accelerants that draw attention to a trend but are not the underlying cause of a genuine supercycle.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If true, investors should position for a multi-year structural commodity uptrend rather than trading each geopolitical headline as a one-off spike.
insights.quote: I don't think that the straight of Hormuz is a catalyst for a super cycle.

insights.claim: Outside of copper and lithium, meaningful new investment capital still has not flowed into oil, natural gas, or uranium projects despite years of price strength, which Adam Rosenwik sees as evidence the commodity upcycle is still in its early innings.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Sectors starved of new capex (oil, gas, uranium) may have more room to run than commodities like copper where investment has already resumed.
insights.quote: this has a long long way to go and I think it's just getting started.

insights.claim: Persistent underinvestment in commodity production has been extended beyond a typical cycle by two structural forces: years of ESG-driven capital avoidance of resource sectors, and a 'carry trade' that has pulled money into large-cap, high-multiple, low-volatility growth assets instead of commodities.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: This capital-allocation feedback loop implies commodity producers could stay capital-starved (and prices structurally higher) until the carry trade unwinds.

themes.theme: Hormuz Closure: A Real Shock vs 2022's Phantom Disruption

insights.claim: In the 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict, Russian crude and refined product exports were never actually disrupted -- Russia rerouted sales to India, China and other buyers via shadow markets -- so the oil price spike to $120 was driven by fear rather than a real supply loss, unlike the 2025 Strait of Hormuz closure which caused an actual physical shut-in of production.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Distinguishing feared disruptions from real ones explains why 2022's oil spike reversed quickly while the 2025 shock could prove more durable.

insights.claim: Of the roughly 20 million barrels per day that normally transits the Strait of Hormuz, only about 5 million barrels per day could be rerouted through East-West bypass pipelines in Saudi Arabia, UAE and Iraq, leaving roughly 15 million barrels per day of upstream production shut in for about 100 days.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: A 15 million b/d, 100-day shut-in is a genuinely massive physical supply loss that dwarfs the 2022 Russia scare, implying the oil market reaction should eventually be much larger.
insights.quote: Of that 5 million or so is now getting out through East West bypass pipelines in Saudi Arabia, UAE and Iraq.

insights.claim: The Hormuz closure withheld between roughly 1 billion and 1.5 billion barrels of oil from the global market over the ~100 days it lasted.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: This is the scale of supply loss that has to be absorbed by draining inventories, and it anchors the later math on how much further inventories must fall.
insights.quote: you had between a billion and a billion and a half barrels impacted and that is just absolutely massive

insights.claim: There is a roughly 60-90 day physical lag between an upstream oil supply shock and its appearance in inventory data, because oil must move through pumping, shipping, storage, refining and distribution before it shows up in reported stocks -- meaning today's inventory data reflects conditions from roughly two months earlier.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Traders dismissing the bullish fundamental story because inventories 'haven't collapsed yet' may be using stale data that lags the real supply shock by 6-8 weeks.

themes.theme: The Missing Barrels: Inventory Math and the Summer Reckoning

insights.claim: US commercial plus strategic petroleum inventories were already down about 240 million barrels from their peak, and if the market is only halfway through working off the Hormuz-closure supply shock, the total US drawdown could reach roughly 500 million barrels, implying a global inventory draw near 1 billion barrels.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: A projected 500-million-barrel US drawdown gives investors a concrete inventory target to track for confirming or invalidating the bullish oil thesis.
insights.quote: in the US right now, I think inventories from their peak are down about 240 million barrels or so

insights.claim: Of the roughly 7-8 billion barrels of headline global oil inventory, only about 1 billion barrels is genuinely usable working capital -- after excluding roughly 2 billion barrels permanently trapped as pipeline line-fill, roughly 1.7 billion barrels always in transit on tankers, and the unreachable bottom 10% of every storage tank -- so a JPMorgan analysis pegs system stress starting around a 700-million-barrel draw and catastrophic failure around 1.2 billion barrels.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Headline inventory figures vastly overstate the oil market's real spare buffer; investors should track the roughly 1-billion-barrel usable pool, not the 8-billion-barrel gross figure, to gauge how close the system is to breaking.
insights.quote: somewhere between 700 million and 1.2 billion is where you start to 700 million you start to develop pressure in the system and 1.2 you have a catastrophic failure of the system

insights.claim: Because it took about 6-8 weeks for inventories to start visibly declining after the Strait of Hormuz closed, Adam Rosenwik expects a symmetric ~8-week lag before inventories start rebuilding even after the strait reopens, meaning the market is only about halfway through its inventory draw and could face a severe shortage around the end of summer.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Investors should expect inventories to keep falling for weeks even after the strait reopens, which could catch bearish positioning offside into late summer.
insights.quote: I don't think we have enough in the tanks to get us there. So, I think we're going to have a [music] huge, huge problem potentially at the end of the summer

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik expects clarity on the true market impact of the Hormuz closure within the next three to four months, and states that if no impact has materialized by then, the bullish oil thesis should be reassessed.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: This gives investors a concrete falsifiability window (3-4 months) for testing the bullish oil thesis against actual inventory and price data.
insights.quote: within 3 or 4 months, it will be. And I think we're going to have a very very different view of of of energy markets after that

themes.theme: Strategic Reserves Running Dry: US, OECD, and China

insights.claim: The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve stands at about 319 million barrels, down roughly 100 million barrels over the last few months and at its lowest level since 1982, with Adam Rosenwik estimating only about 50-60 million more barrels can be drawn before the reserve's salt-dome storage hits operationally problematic pressure limits.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: The US SPR is close to running out of usable spare capacity as a price-suppression tool, removing a lever policymakers have used to offset supply shocks.
insights.quote: right now you're down at 319 million barrels

insights.claim: OECD countries agreed to release about 400 million barrels of strategic reserves in total but are only about halfway through delivery and running behind schedule, and Adam Rosenwik doubts there is another large tranche of releases coming, partly because the largely US-funded OECD had an incentive to announce a maximal headline figure early to calm the market regardless of whether it would fully deliver.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If OECD SPR releases are capped near 400 million barrels and running behind, that removes an expected source of supply relief investors may be pricing in.
insights.quote: there's a lot of reason to believe that 400 is on the upper end of what they can possibly do

insights.claim: China is the only major non-OECD country with a materially sized strategic petroleum reserve, opaquely estimated at roughly 1 billion barrels, built specifically as insurance against a scenario where a US-led move to shut the Straits of Malacca and Hormuz during a Taiwan conflict leaves China energy-vulnerable.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: China's roughly 1-billion-barrel SPR gives it meaningfully more strategic buffer than markets may appreciate, complicating forecasts of how a prolonged energy shock would play out.
insights.quote: they've built up kind of a billion barrels or so, a fairly large SPR uh figure

themes.theme: China's Refined-Product Export Ban as a Taiwan War Game

insights.claim: The day the Strait of Hormuz closed, China banned refined-product exports (a policy still in effect) which paradoxically didn't reduce its crude import needs proportionally -- instead it pushed idled-refinery crude back into the spot market -- a move Adam Rosenwik interprets as a deliberate rehearsal of how China would manage energy security in a Taiwan conflict scenario, not an economically optimal decision.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If China's export ban is a geopolitical signal rather than a market response, investors should expect it to persist independent of price incentives, keeping global refined-product markets tight.
insights.quote: I personally think if you look at what China has done since the start of the Hormuz crisis, it's a little bit of a war games or a trial run of what they would do in the event of going into Taiwan.

insights.claim: Reported global oil demand-destruction figures of 5-6 million barrels per day (comparable in scale to COVID-19) are, in Adam Rosenwik's view, largely a modeling artifact of China's refinery shutdowns feeding into refinery-run-based demand estimates, not a genuine collapse in end-user consumption -- actual indicators like packed airports and rising vehicle miles show no COVID-like weakness.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If demand is not actually falling, the market is under-drawing inventories relative to true consumption, making the bullish supply-shortage case stronger than headline demand data suggests.
insights.quote: The airports are packed. I can't get a seat on half the flights

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik argues energy efficiency matters as much as diversification: Germany's shift away from nuclear toward renewables has coincided with a loss of manufacturing competitiveness, while the US's more hydrocarbon- and market-driven energy mix has delivered both the largest CO2 reduction and the biggest efficiency gains -- implying China's renewable and nuclear buildout will only reduce oil demand 'on the margin' for the next 15-20 years.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Investors shouldn't assume China's alternative-energy buildout will materially cut its oil demand in the medium term; hydrocarbons remain structurally necessary for efficiency.
insights.quote: Germany has decided to go down a renewable path and get rid of its nukes and go towards renewables

insights.claim: China is not capable of fundamentally restructuring its energy consumption within the next 12-18 months; Adam Rosenwik believes genuine structural change in China's oil demand would take five years or more, meaning its current refined-product export halt is a short/medium-term stockpile-management tactic, not evidence of a long-term demand shift.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Investors betting on a structural collapse in Chinese oil demand from its energy diversification push are likely mistiming the story by years.
insights.quote: China is not in a position today to fundamentally change how it consumes energy

themes.theme: Positioning, the Ceasefire Myth, and Persistent Bearish Selling

insights.claim: Investor and speculator positioning in energy was 'super super short' heading into the Hormuz crisis around March 2, to the point that several large multi-strategy hedge funds reportedly fired their entire energy trading desks in early March, just before the conflict escalated.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Extreme pre-crisis short positioning explains both the initial short-covering rally and the market's subsequent skepticism, suggesting sentiment (not fundamentals) has driven much of the price action.
insights.quote: positioning of investors and speculators going into March 2nd was super short, super super short

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik disputes the market narrative of a full round-trip in oil sentiment, arguing investors were bearish throughout the entire episode -- covering shorts briefly for a couple of weeks (producing the initial rally) before bearish selling pressure resumed and accelerated once a perceived ceasefire took hold.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If the market never actually turned bullish, current bearish positioning may be more stretched and fragile than it appears, raising the risk of another sharp squeeze if inventories keep collapsing.

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik believes there is no real ceasefire in the Iran conflict despite market pricing -- he argues neither side genuinely wants one, and the 'ceasefire' narrative is being sustained mainly because of the upcoming US midterm elections.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If the ceasefire is more political theater than reality, renewed escalation risk is materially underpriced in oil markets.
insights.quote: The only reason the narrative is even there is because of the midterms

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik's fund has held a longstanding view that global oil demand has been meaningfully stronger than consensus appreciated, which is why inventories stayed roughly stable last year rather than building the way they would have in the large surplus most forecasters assumed existed.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: A demand base case stronger than consensus implies less cushion in the system, making today's inventory draws more structurally significant rather than a one-off shock.
insights.quote: our long-term view has always been demand has been a little bit stronger, quite a bit stronger than most people appreciate

themes.theme: Gold: Why Adam Rotated Out Near the Top and What Would Bring Him Back

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik's fund exited nearly all of its gold mining equity positions in two tranches -- a small valuation-driven sale in October when the gold-to-oil ratio looked stretched, and the bulk in January, within about three days of gold's eventual price top -- rotating the proceeds into energy equities.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: A well-timed rotation out of gold into energy by a natural-resources specialist fund signals a relative-value shift worth tracking for other commodity allocators.
insights.quote: we we owe the timing gods one on that one because we we got that one pretty good within about 3 days at the top of of gold there

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik's January decision to exit gold was driven by several converging signals: a parabolic catch-up rally in silver (historically a marker that precious-metals rallies are ending), gold's own parabolic price action, a surge in 'fast money' gold ETF accumulation, and a market pricing in three rate cuts that he viewed as overly dovish given a hawkish Fed backdrop, creating 'buy the rumor, sell the news' risk.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: This gives investors a repeatable checklist (silver catch-up, ETF inflow surge, dovish rate positioning) for spotting precious-metals blow-off tops.
insights.quote: silver staged this massive catch-up rally

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik frames the current gold drawdown as a normal bull-market consolidation rather than a trend change, noting historical precedents in 1973-74 and 2008-09 saw 35-40% pullbacks lasting 2-3 years, and the current pullback is only about halfway to that historical depth.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: If gold is only mid-way through a historically typical consolidation, investors shouldn't expect a durable trend reversal for another 1-2+ years even if the current selloff feels severe.
insights.quote: 35%'s not uncommon. 40% is not unheard of

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik's contrarian signal for re-entering gold is sentiment-based: he says he'll get interested again 'when everyone stops asking' him to buy gold, since the current widespread desire among his audience for him to rotate back into gold is itself evidence the market hasn't bottomed.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Persistent bullish gold sentiment despite the drawdown suggests capitulation hasn't happened yet, arguing against a near-term bottom.
insights.quote: when everyone stops asking me, that's when it'll be time to get back into gold

themes.theme: Chinaification of US Strategic Capital and the Real AI Energy Bottleneck

insights.claim: The host argues the US is undertaking a 'Chinaification' of its economic model -- building direct government equity stakes in strategic companies via a sovereign wealth fund and 'Trump accounts' -- that could scale toward 5-10% government ownership of strategic US equities within 10 years, funded through dollar issuance and expected to accelerate after the midterms into next year.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If the US government becomes a large direct equity investor in strategic sectors, that changes capital allocation dynamics for commodities and infrastructure well beyond AI.
insights.quote: I think of this as the Chinaification of of the American system.

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik expects US government strategic investment to concentrate in rare earths (via offtake agreements that set price floors to de-risk new capital), uranium mining and the fuel-enrichment/fabrication cycle, and copper -- though he's skeptical these will prove economically optimal long-term investments even though he thinks they're politically likely.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Government-backed price floors and offtake deals in rare earths and uranium could de-risk equity investment in those names even if the underlying economics remain questionable.
insights.quote: I suspect that copper uh will be a fairly important strategic mineral for the government

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik describes copper as 'the antithesis of oil': despite prices near all-time highs and near-universal investor bullishness, mine supply grew quickly last year (contrary to consensus that supply can't grow), Chinese demand has stagnated for about 18 months, and inventories have swollen to 30-year highs -- making current valuations look completely disconnected from fundamentals.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Despite government interest in copper as a strategic metal, Adam sees it as fundamentally overvalued today relative to oil, a potential caution signal for commodity investors.
insights.quote: we look at the copper market and in many ways it's the antithesis of oil

insights.claim: Adam Rosenwik's highest-conviction call is that the biggest opportunity in the market today is not AI chips or specialty metals but the upstream natural gas supply needed to power AI data centers -- an area almost nobody is investing in even though the incremental energy demand is 'astronomical.'
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Investors chasing AI exposure through chips or exotic metals may be overlooking the far larger and underinvested upstream energy supply chain that will physically constrain data center buildout.
insights.quote: Nobody wants to invest in the molecule of natural gas, let's say, that will inevitably power this data center. That's the biggest opportunity that exists in the market today.

insights.claim: Even market participants focused on AI's power needs are misdirecting capital toward building power plants -- which merely convert energy into electricity -- rather than toward the upstream energy source itself, which is the actual bottleneck.
insights.speaker: Adam Rosenwik
insights.so_what: Distinguishing 'energy' (the molecule) from 'power infrastructure' (the plant) points investors toward upstream gas supply and pipeline ownership as the underappreciated trade, not just power generation assets.
insights.quote: what they really want is they they want to build the power plant but that's not energy
